content.problemStatement:
Let us solve some more Sorting based problems

Problem statementYou are given an array of NNN integers. Find the minimum number of integers you need to delete from the array such that the absolute difference between each pair of integers in the remaining array will become equal.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing one integer - the minimum number of integers to be deleted to satisfy the given condition.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases fits in typical memory limits
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N (the size of the array). The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum number of elements that must be deleted so that the absolute difference between every pair of remaining integers is the same.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: both numbers are different, we can keep the two elements, deletions = 0.
Test case 2: the value 2 appears three times, keep those three, delete the other two.
Test case 3: the most frequent value appears twice, keep those two equal numbers, delete the remaining two.
All three numbers are distinct. We can keep any two of them (difference between the single pair is constant), so we delete 1 element.
All numbers are already equal, no deletions needed.

hidden.label: edge case: two distinct values, many repetitions

solution.approach:
For the remaining numbers to satisfy the condition, either all of them are equal (difference 0) or there are exactly two numbers (any two values give only one pair, so the condition is trivially satisfied). Therefore, for an array of size N we can keep either:
1. All occurrences of the most frequent value (count = maxFreq). This works because all kept numbers are equal.
2. Any two elements (if N >= 2). This works because there is only one pair.
The best we can keep is max(maxFreq, 2) (for N >= 2). For N = 1 we keep the single element. The answer is N minus this best keep size.
Algorithm per test case:
- Count frequencies of each value (using a hash map).
- Determine maxFreq.
- If N == 1, answer = 0.
- Else answer = N - max(maxFreq, 2).
Complexities: O(N) time and O(distinct) extra space per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the array.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(D) where D is the number of distinct values (at most N).
